title: The Anthropic Investment Postmortem: Why Huang Calls It His Biggest Strategic Miss

source.original: Jensen Huang – Will Nvidia's moat persist?

body_html:
Jensen Huang is not a CEO who readily admits mistakes, which makes his candid assessment of the Anthropic situation all the more striking. He describes it plainly as a strategic miss: when Anthropic needed billions in compute-linked investment to scale, Nvidia simply wasn't in a position to provide it. Google and AWS were. The result was a lock-in that sent one of the world's leading AI labs onto competing silicon.

The root of the miss, as Huang tells it, was a failure of imagination about the capital structure of frontier AI labs. He assumed Anthropic could raise from traditional venture capital "like all companies do." What he didn't internalize was that building a frontier lab required investment at a scale—$5 to $10 billion—that no VC would underwrite. The hyperscalers understood this before Nvidia did, and they used compute commitments as a binding mechanism for their chip platforms.

Huang is transparent that even had he understood the dynamics earlier, Nvidia probably couldn't have acted. The company had never made external investments at that scale, and the corporate muscle for it simply didn't exist. But the lesson clearly landed. Nvidia has since invested heavily in OpenAI (reportedly up to $30 billion) and in Anthropic when the opportunity arose. Huang frames these investments not as financial plays but as ecosystem imperatives—ensuring that the world's most important AI labs remain accessible to and built upon the Nvidia stack.

What's most revealing is Huang's refusal to frame Anthropic's existence as bad news, even though it created his most visible competitive loss. "Anthropic's existence is great for the world. I'm delighted for it." This reflects a broader corporate philosophy: Nvidia doesn't pick winners, it supports the ecosystem. The Anthropic miss taught Huang that sometimes supporting the ecosystem requires writing very large checks, and that the cost of inaction can be measured in entire platform shifts.
